questions.question: Which description best characterizes Google Spanner?
options: A scalable, multi-version, globally distributed, synchronously replicated database with SQL and externally consistent transactions
An eventually consistent key-value cache with no transactions
A single-region in-memory store used only for session data
A batch analytics engine that replaces MapReduce for offline jobs
questions.explanation: The Spanner paper defines it as a multi-version, globally distributed, synchronously replicated database that shards data across Paxos state machines and supports externally consistent distributed transactions with a SQL query language.

questions.question: What makes the TrueTime API different from a normal system clock?
options: TT.now() returns an interval [earliest, latest] that is guaranteed to contain the true absolute time, explicitly exposing clock uncertainty
It returns a single exact nanosecond value with zero error
It only works on one machine and cannot be compared across datacenters
It replaces Paxos so replication is no longer needed
questions.explanation: TrueTime reifies uncertainty: TT.now() yields a bounded interval guaranteed to contain the real time. Spanner reasons about ordering using these bounds instead of trusting a single clock reading.

questions.question: TrueTime keeps its uncertainty bound small (generally under ~10 ms) by relying on which time references?
options: GPS receivers and atomic clocks, which have uncorrelated failure modes
A single NTP server per continent
Client-side wall-clock estimates averaged together
The commit timestamps of recent transactions only
questions.explanation: Time-master machines use GPS and atomic clocks precisely because their failure modes are uncorrelated. Daemons poll multiple masters and apply a Marzullo-style algorithm to reject outliers, keeping uncertainty (epsilon) small.

questions.source: Spanner OSDI 2012 paper — external consistency invariant
questions.question: Spanner's external consistency guarantee states that:
options: If transaction T1 commits before T2 starts, then T1's commit timestamp is less than T2's commit timestamp
All transactions are eventually visible in some arbitrary order
Reads may return data from any point in time with no ordering guarantee
Writes never need coordination because clocks are perfectly synchronized
questions.explanation: External consistency (equivalent to linearizability) means the commit-timestamp order matches real-time order: a transaction that commits before another starts is assigned an earlier timestamp.

questions.source: Spanner OSDI 2012 paper — Section 4.1.2 commit wait
questions.question: How does Spanner's 'commit wait' use TrueTime to preserve external consistency?
options: The coordinator waits until TT.after(s) is true — until the commit timestamp s is guaranteed to be in the past — before making the write visible
It waits a fixed 10 seconds after every commit regardless of clock uncertainty
It skips waiting entirely and relies on clients to retry
It waits only for the slowest disk flush, not for time to pass
questions.explanation: After choosing commit timestamp s from TT.now().latest, the coordinator waits out the uncertainty until TT.after(s) holds. This guarantees s is in the absolute past, so later transactions get strictly larger timestamps. Expected wait is about 2*epsilon.

questions.question: How does Spanner replicate a split (tablet) across failure domains?
options: Each split is managed by a Paxos group; a majority of voting replicas must agree, and one replica is elected leader to handle writes
Each split is copied lazily with no consensus, accepting divergence
A single primary machine holds the only copy of each split
Splits are replicated only within one rack for speed
questions.explanation: Spanner runs a Paxos state machine per tablet. As long as a majority of voting replicas are up, a leader is elected to process writes while sufficiently up-to-date replicas can serve reads.

questions.source: Spanner OSDI 2012 paper — Paxos leader leases
questions.question: Spanner's Paxos leaders hold time-based leader leases (10 seconds by default). What is the key invariant these leases enforce?
options: Disjointness: each Paxos leader's lease interval is disjoint from every other leader's, so timestamp assignment stays monotonic across leaders
That every replica becomes leader in round-robin order
That leases never expire so failover is unnecessary
That reads must always go through the previous leader
questions.explanation: Long-lived leases make leadership stable and enforce the disjointness invariant: no two leaders' lease intervals overlap. This lets Spanner assign monotonically increasing timestamps even across leader changes.

questions.question: In Spanner, what is a 'directory' (bucket)?
options: A set of contiguous keys sharing a common prefix — the unit of data placement and movement between Paxos groups
A filesystem folder on each spanserver's local disk
A cache of recently read rows held only in memory
A list of all users assigned to a datacenter
questions.explanation: A directory is a bucket of contiguous keys with a shared prefix. It is the smallest unit whose placement and geographic replication can be specified, and Movedir relocates data directory-by-directory (actually by fragment) between Paxos groups.

questions.source: Spanner OSDI 2012 paper — Section 2.1 spanserver stack
questions.question: Where is a Spanner tablet's state physically stored?
options: In B-tree-like files and a write-ahead log on the Colossus distributed file system
Only in the leader's local RAM with no durable log
In a single global SQL master database
On the client machine that issued the write
questions.explanation: Each spanserver hosts 100–1000 tablets whose state lives in B-tree-like files plus a write-ahead log on Colossus (the GFS successor). Spanner layers Paxos, a lock table, and a transaction manager on top of this Bigtable-derived stack.

questions.question: When a read-write transaction spans multiple Paxos groups, how does Spanner commit it atomically?
options: The groups' leaders run two-phase commit layered over Paxos, with one participant group acting as coordinator
It abandons atomicity and commits each group independently
It forces all groups to merge into one machine first
It uses a single global lock that blocks the whole database
questions.explanation: Single-group transactions bypass the transaction manager (Paxos + lock table suffice). Multi-group transactions use two-phase commit across participant leaders, coordinated by one chosen group, with each phase durably logged through Paxos.

questions.question: Why can Spanner's read-only transactions execute without acquiring locks?
options: They run as lock-free snapshot reads at a system-chosen timestamp, so incoming writes are never blocked
They lock the entire database for the duration of the read
They only ever read a single row at a time
They disable replication while running
questions.explanation: Read-only transactions are predeclared as write-free and execute at a chosen timestamp as snapshot reads on any sufficiently up-to-date replica. This avoids locking and does not block concurrent writes.

questions.question: Which concurrency-control mechanism do Spanner read-write transactions use?
options: Pessimistic two-phase locking at the leader, with wound-wait to avoid deadlocks
Optimistic concurrency control with no locks at all
Timestamp-only ordering with no locks, like Calvin
Single global serialization on one coordinator node
questions.explanation: Read-write transactions acquire read/write locks at the leader using two-phase locking and use wound-wait to prevent deadlocks. Writes are buffered at the client until commit.

questions.question: How is a Spanner deployment organized at the top level?
options: A universe contains zones (units of physical isolation and replication); each zone has a zonemaster and many spanservers
A single flat pool of identical servers with no zones
One server per continent connected directly to clients
A hierarchy of CDNs with no persistent storage tier
questions.explanation: A Spanner deployment is a universe organized into zones — the unit of administrative deployment and physical isolation. A zone has a zonemaster (assigns data to spanservers) and 100s–1000s of spanservers; a placement driver moves data between zones.

questions.question: In CAP terms, how is Spanner best characterized?
options: A CP system: it preserves consistency during partitions and relies on Paxos majorities plus TrueTime for very high availability in practice
An AP system that always accepts writes on any replica during a partition
A system that abandons partition tolerance entirely
A cache with no consistency guarantees
questions.explanation: Spanner chooses consistency over availability under partitions (CP). Because writes need a Paxos majority and external consistency depends on TrueTime, a minority-partitioned replica cannot accept conflicting writes — but majority quorums keep practical availability very high.

questions.question: Why did Google's F1 advertising backend migrate from sharded MySQL to Spanner?
options: To eliminate painful manual resharding while gaining synchronous replication, automatic failover, and strong transactional semantics
Because Spanner was eventually consistent and cheaper to operate
Because F1 needed a single-datacenter cache with no transactions
Because Spanner removed the need for any schema or SQL
questions.explanation: F1's sharded MySQL required multi-year resharding efforts and had risky failover. Spanner removed manual resharding and provided synchronous Paxos replication, automatic failover, and strong transactions across arbitrary data.
